EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men were struck by lightning as they sat under a tree to avoid a heavy downpour.   One of the men was unconscious and had no pulse when emergency responders arrived. He was immediately taken to Denver Health was listed in critical condition.  The other victim suffered less severe injuries. He was treated at the scene and hospitalized, then released a few hours later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men were injured when lightning hit the tree they were sitting under during the downpour.  One man was critically injured, while the other suffered only minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy snow to the North Central Mountains, then brought strong winds and heavy snow to the northeast plains of Colorado.  In the mountains, storm totals included:  15 inches at Cabin Creek; 12.5 inches, 3 miles east-northeast of Tabernash; 12 inches at Winter Park, 11 inches at Rawah SNOTEL; 10 inches at Echo Lake; 8 inches at Deadman Hill and Willow Park.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado, storm totals included:  9 inches in Peetz; 8 inches in Brush and 5 miles west-southwest of Leroy; 7 inches at Fort Morgan, 4 miles west-northwest of Iliff and Sterling; 6.5 inches, 4 miles northwest of Elizabeth; 6 inches, 11 miles south-southeast of Haxtun and Woodrow; 5 inches, 21 miles north of New Raymer; with 4 inches in Julesburg.  In Peetz, strong winds produced snow drifts, up to 4 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One snowshoer was killed but two others survived when an avalanche swept down a One man was killed as he and to others were hiking along Kelso Ridge near the town of Bakerville, about 45 miles west of Denver.  A female snowshoer was leading the group across a suspicious area on the mountain, at about 12 thousand feet, when she turned around and saw that an avalanche had hit, burying one of her two male companions.  The woman and the man not caught up in the slide located the buried man, who was equipped with an avalanche beacon, about 20 minutes later.  He was not breathing when they found him.

A line of strong thunderstorms developed over Colorado and swept east during the evening. Penny to quarter size hail fell in Yuma with a 73 mph wind gust reported in far western Cheyenne County northeast of Aroya.

Precipitation for the month was close to normal in Cheyenne County with observers reporting anywhere from a third of an inch below normal to nearly an inch above normal for June.  This brought a slight easing of drought conditions for the county. At the beginning of June the area was rated as being in Moderate (D2) to Severe (D3) Drought.  By month's end, that rating improved one category, ranging from Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) Drought with the worst conditions remaining in southern portions of Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought rating for the county improved slightly with Moderate (D1) Drought in the north and Severe (D2) Drought in the south. The highest rainfall total in the county as reported from cooperative observers was 0.98 inches above normal near Wild Horse. Cheyenne Wells and Arapahoe in the eastern part of the county were drier than normal with observers reporting 0.22 and 0.34 below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms broke out across the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains.  Large hail, ranging in size from 1 to 2 inches, was observed.  The area extended from around  Englewood to Aurora.   As many as 212 thousand residences were potentially impacted by this storm in and around the Denver area.   The hail broke shattered windshields and damaged vehicles.  One tornado touched down briefly in an open field.

A storm system that moved through the area produced large hail, up to golfball size, and street flooding in parts of the metro area then spread east into the plains.  The hailstorm was the most costly of the summer season with with insured losses topping $213.3 million, according to the Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Association.|Insurance claims included 29,297 automobile claims worth more than $87.2 million and 14,287 property claims for $126 million, ranking the storm as the eighth most expensive to hit the state.  The storm also caused multiple accidents. A semi became detached from a trailer, blocking traffic on westbound Interstate 70 west of Tower Road.  Downed power poles blocked a roadway on CO 79 near Bennett, at mile marker 10.  In addition to the damaging winds, the storms produced large hail, from nickel to golfball size.  The intense thunderstorm winds also downed trees near Fort Morgan.  Flash flooding was reported over parts of northern Washington County.  The combination of flash flooding and an accident involving a semi-trailer forced the closure of CO 61 for a several hours.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow in parts of the North Central Mountains, heaviest  north of Interstate 70.   Eldora Ski Area measured 14 inches of snow, with 11.5 inches, 4 miles southeast of Gould.  Heavy snow fell over parts of the Front Range Foothills as well.  Trained spotters in Genesee and 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs measured 10 inches of snowfall.  Elsewhere, storm totals ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jetstream coupled with abundant moisture from the Pacific brought an extended period of heavy snow to the North Central Mountains of Colorado where enhanced banded snowfall allowed storm totals to exceed 2 feet over a 3-day period.  Across the Front Range Foothills, strong downslope winds in excess of 80 mph preceded the storm.  Locally heavy snowfall eventually spilled over into northern foothills and adjacent plains.  Across the plains, the heaviest snowfall generally occurred north of Interstate 70.  Storm totals across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains ranged from 5 to 11 inches, with the heaviest snowfall in central Weld and northern Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  15 inches at Lake Irene, 12 inches at Wild Basin and 9 inches at Phantom Valley.

Active monsoonal thunderstorms with heavy rainfall produced flash flooding across portions of west central Colorado during the afternoon and early evening hours of September 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed along the washes in western Mesa County including Salt Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall produced localized flash flooding and urban flooding in  and around Durango during the early morning hours of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and urban flooding from heavy rainfall occurred in Durango and north of town.  Highway 550 was closed north of town due to fast flowing water and rockslides over the roadway.  Manhole covers where also blown off in portions of the city due to heavy runoff which overwhelmed storm drains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved through the foothills of the San Juan mountains near Pagosa Springs during the afternoon of September 29th. The strongest storm moved through eastern Archuleta County which produced damaging outflow winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of trees were blown down by thunderstorm outflow winds in the San Juan National Forest, including a large area near County Road 302 in eastern Archuleta County.  Live trees up to 3 feet in diameter were snapped off like toothpicks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved through the foothills of the San Juan mountains near Pagosa Springs during the afternoon of September 29th. The strongest storm moved through eastern Archuleta County which produced damaging outflow winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball sized hail was reported by several members of the public just east of Pagosa Springs in a sparsely populated location.  Some damage occurred to vehicles.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LAKE COUNTY / WESTERN MOSQUITO RANGE ABOVE 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced heavy snow at times, as well as gusty winds to portions of southern Colorado.  Some of the higher reported snow totals with this event include:  6 inches near Saguache (Saguache County)...Monument (El Paso County)...and Maysville (Chaffee County)...and 15 to 21 inches around Leadville and the surrounding mountains.

A storm system that moved through the area produced large hail, up to golfball size, and street flooding in parts of the metro area then spread east into the plains.  The hailstorm was the most costly of the summer season with with insured losses topping $213.3 million, according to the Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Association.|Insurance claims included 29,297 automobile claims worth more than $87.2 million and 14,287 property claims for $126 million, ranking the storm as the eighth most expensive to hit the state.  The storm also caused multiple accidents. A semi became detached from a trailer, blocking traffic on westbound Interstate 70 west of Tower Road.  Downed power poles blocked a roadway on CO 79 near Bennett, at mile marker 10.  In addition to the damaging winds, the storms produced large hail, from nickel to golfball size.  The intense thunderstorm winds also downed trees near Fort Morgan.  Flash flooding was reported over parts of northern Washington County.  The combination of flash flooding and an accident involving a semi-trailer forced the closure of CO 61 for a several hours.

During the late afternoon through the early morning on the following day, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall setup along the Kansas-Nebraska border, stretching west into the foothills of the Rockies.  The band intensified during the evening and lasted through almost sunrise before dissipating.  There was almost no wind with this snowfall, which is rare for this area.  Total snowfall accumulations for Yuma county ranged from around 5 to 13 inches, with the majority of the county in the 8-13 inch range.  The highest snowfall amount was reported at Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement measured 7 at Yuma at 12:15 AM MST, with a total snowfall amount of 13 inches by the following morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Kit Carson.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jetstream coupled with abundant moisture from the Pacific brought an extended period of heavy snow to the North Central Mountains of Colorado where enhanced banded snowfall allowed storm totals to exceed 2 feet over a 3-day period.  Across the Front Range Foothills, strong downslope winds in excess of 80 mph preceded the storm.  Locally heavy snowfall eventually spilled over into northern foothills and adjacent plains.  Across the plains, the heaviest snowfall generally occurred north of Interstate 70.  Storm totals across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains ranged from 5 to 11 inches, with the heaviest snowfall in central Weld and northern Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  15 inches at Columbine, 14 inches at Tower, 11 inches at Zirkel  and 10 inches at Buffalo Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly flow aloft produced high winds over the northeast plains of Colorado.  Peak wind gusts included 59 mph in Sterling and 58 mph at Fort Morgan Municipal Airport.  The strong winds reduced the visibility to near zero at times in blowing dust.  The poor visibility was blamed on multiple accidents along on U.S. 34 near Akron.  As a result, the highway was closed in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather disturbance and its associated cold front moved across northern Colorado during the late morning and afternoon; spawning several tornadoes, damaging wind, large hail, very heavy rainfall.  Nine tornadoes touched down across northeast Colorado. Three of the tornadoes occurred in Park County, at elevations of 8,000-10,000 feet. The first tornado occurred 8 miles south-southwest of Fairplay; it damaged the roof of a residence and was assigned a rating of EF-1. The second tornado developed 6 miles southeast of Fairplay in open country. The third tornado developed 4 miles west of Lake George; it was given a rating of EF-2. The last tornado caused damage to some homes and overturned several recreational vehicles at an RV park. Several power lines were also downed and some buildings in the town of Lake George were damaged, and trees were also snapped from their bases.  This tornado then passed into Teller County.  ||In Aurora, another tornado touched down near the Blackstone Country Club, it lifted some golf carts and flipped an empty construction trailer. One of the carts was thrown on top of a caddy walking nearby; he was taken to a hospital with minor injuries. It was assigned an EF-1 rating. Five other tornadoes developed over open country across Douglas, Elbert and Weld Counties with little additional damage reported.  In Englewood, lightning struck a tree which damaged two nearby homes and a truck.  Large hail, up to half dollar size, very heavy rain, and minor flooding was also reported.

During the morning and into the afternoon strong winds gusting 35 MPH to near 60 MPH were reported along with blowing snow, reducing visibilities to near zero at times.  The highest gusts reported were in Cheyenne County.  The slick road conditions and poor visibilities caused multiple car accidents on I-70, with 2 reported injury crashes.  As a result I-70 was closed from Limon to Goodland until the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities dropped to less than a quarter mile periodically during the overnight and into the afternoon, mainly from Stratton to the stateline.  Many accidents were reported on I-70, including 4 injury crashes.  One semi tipped over and four or five semis jack-knifed.  CDOT closed I-70 from Limon to the state line for seven and a half hours.

The first tornado occurred 8 miles south-southwest of Fairplay; it damaged the roof of a residence and was assigned a rating of EF-1. The second tornado developed 6 miles southeast of Fairplay in open country. The third tornado developed 4 miles west of Lake George; it was given a rating of EF-2. The last tornado caused damage to some homes and overturned several recreational vehicles at an RV park. Several power lines were also downed and some buildings in the town of Lake George were damaged, and trees were also snapped from their bases.  This tornado then passed into Teller County.  ||In Aurora, another tornado touched down near the Blackstone Country Club, it lifted some golf carts and flipped an empty construction trailer. One of the carts was thrown on top of a caddy walking nearby; he was taken to a hospital with minor injuries. It was assigned an EF-1 rating. Five other tornadoes developed over open country across Douglas, Elbert and Weld Counties with little additional damage reported.  In Englewood, lightning struck a tree which damaged two nearby homes and a truck.  Large hail, up to half dollar size, very heavy rain, and minor flooding was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in an open field; no damage was observed.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Southwest monsoon continued to direct moisture into the Great Basin and central Rockies through September. Outside of two outlier drought zones, the bulk of the climate stations across western Colorado showed an excess of precipitation by at least 2/3 of an inch.   Nonetheless, significant improvement to the drought was achieved across southwest Colorado near the Four Corners where monthly precipitation totals were near an inch above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to severe drought conditions at the beginning of September were reduced to moderate or better drought conditions by the end of the month. This improvement was due to widespread and above normal precipitation that was received across the area in a persistent monsoonal pattern.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active monsoonal thunderstorms with heavy rainfall produced flash flooding across portions of west central Colorado during the afternoon and early evening hours of September 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall filled a reservoir and resulted in the overtopping of a privately owned dam which resulted in flash flood waters up to 5 feet deep below the dam.  The top two to three feet of the dam was eroded by the water flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather disturbance and its associated cold front moved across northern Colorado during the late morning and afternoon; spawning several tornadoes, damaging wind, large hail, very heavy rainfall.  Nine tornadoes touched down across northeast Colorado. Three of the tornadoes occurred in Park County, at elevations of 8,000-10,000 feet. The first tornado occurred 8 miles south-southwest of Fairplay; it damaged the roof of a residence and was assigned a rating of EF-1. The second tornado developed 6 miles southeast of Fairplay in open country. The third tornado developed 4 miles west of Lake George; it was given a rating of EF-2. The last tornado caused damage to some homes and overturned several recreational vehicles at an RV park. Several power lines were also downed and some buildings in the town of Lake George were damaged, and trees were also snapped from their bases.  This tornado then passed into Teller County.  ||In Aurora, another tornado touched down near the Blackstone Country Club, it lifted some golf carts and flipped an empty construction trailer. One of the carts was thrown on top of a caddy walking nearby; he was taken to a hospital with minor injuries. It was assigned an EF-1 rating. Five other tornadoes developed over open country across Douglas, Elbert and Weld Counties with little additional damage reported.  In Englewood, lightning struck a tree which damaged two nearby homes and a truck.  Large hail, up to half dollar size, very heavy rain, and minor flooding was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in open country; no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow and very strong winds in the mountains of northern Colorado.  Peak wind gusts to nearly 100 mph were recorded above timberline atop Loveland Pass.  In addition, storm totals exceeded 2 feet in some locations.  In the mountains, storm totals included:  30 inches, 9 miles east-northeast of Steamboat Springs; 22 inches near Breckenridge and 11 miles south of Rabbit Ears Pass; 20 inches near Copper Mountain; 16 inches near Cameron and Loveland Passes; 15 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Trail Ridge; 14 inches, 5 miles south-southwest of Blue River; 13 inches near Berthoud Pass; 12 inches near Guanella Pass and Long Draw Pass.  Lesser amounts of 6 to 11 inches were recorded.  Peak wind gusts included:  98 mph atop Loveland Pass; 84 mph atop Berthoud Pass and 79 mph at Breckenridge Ski Area.  ||Strong winds were also observed across parts of the east central plains of Colorado as the system exited the state.  Peak wind gusts included 67 mph at Limon Airport and 61 mph at Hugo.

During the morning and into the afternoon strong winds gusting 35 MPH to near 60 MPH were reported along with blowing snow, reducing visibilities to near zero at times.  The highest gusts reported were in Cheyenne County.  The slick road conditions and poor visibilities caused multiple car accidents on I-70, with 2 reported injury crashes.  As a result I-70 was closed from Limon to Goodland until the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported several locations in the county with zero visibility during the day, MM 432 on Hwy 40 near Wild Horse, the intersections of CR W and Hwy 385 and CR Z and Hwy 385.  Once I-70 was closed, traffic increased on Hwy 40.  Officers slowed traffic down on Hwy 40 to help prevent any accidents or slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed over Cheyenne county Colorado during the late morning.  Wind gusts near 60 MPH were reported at Gunderson Farms and Kit Carson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported from a site in Kit Carson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The south half of Cheyenne county remained in D2 drought status for the month.  COOP precipitation amounts ranged from less than half an inch below normal to less than an inch above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts for the county ranged from -0.35 below to 0.86 above normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon high winds moved across portions of east central Colorado.  A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was recorded at the Burlington airport.  The high winds created wide spread blowing dust.  A 10 ft. drift of tumble weeds reportedly blocked a rural road in Cheyenne county.
Wind gusts of 45-55 MPH were recorded during the afternoon across East Central Colorado behind a cold that had moved through earlier in the day. The highest gust reported was 58 MPH at Wray.

During the late evening a strong cold front moved south out of Nebraska into Eastern Colorado.  Behind the front northerly winds briefly increased, with wind gusts of 59 MPH reported at the Wray airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the Wray airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning temperatures fell to around -12F, with light winds around 5 MPH causing wind chills to fall to -25F for parts of Yuma county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Yuma.  Several other sites around the county had wind chill values of -20F to -23F.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow, produced around one inch in less than 30 minutes, contributed to a chain of accidents in the northbound lanes of Interstate 25, between Logan Street and University Boulevard.   The combination of excessive speed and very poor driving conditions led the chain reaction; it involved 104 vehicles and resulted in one death along with 30 injuries.  The interstate was closed for approximately 5 hours.

A moist and moderate southwest flow ahead of an upper low produced significant to heavy snowfall and gusty winds in the mountains of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event which began on the last day of February and continued into March produced snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet across the area. Locally heavier amounts up to 28 inches were reported. Wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow. Even stronger winds on ridgetops included a gust to 82 mph on Eagle Mountain.

Wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow. Locally stronger winds include gusts to 67 mph at the Whitewater Creek observation station near the 10,000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche caught two people in the mountains near Crested Butte, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two backcountry skiers triggered and were caught in an avalanche on Mount Owens in the Ruby Range west of Crested Butte. The two were on foot, climbing a north-facing chute locally know as ���Jenga.��� They were about halfway up when the avalanche broke above them at the 12,600 foot level.  The skiers were caught, carried, and partially buried. They were uninjured but lost some equipment.  The avalanche ran about 400 vertical feet down a slope angle that ranged from 37 to 41 degrees.  The debris field spread about 60 feet wide and ranged from 1 to 5 feet deep.

Another round of high winds developed over much of northeastern Colorado as an intense and nearly stationary storm system sat over northwestern Kansas.  Peak wind gusts included:   67 mph, 4 miles east of Haxtun; 62 mph at Holyoke; and 61 mph at Akron Airport.
Strong surface gradient winds continued Monday night, resulting in a couple isolated high wind reports across eastern Colorado and western Kansas. Wind gusts of 58 and 61 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust recorded at the ASOS site at Kit Carson County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third day of high winds occurred as a slow-moving but intense weather system moved over the Central High Plains. Peak winds of 55 to 65 mph occurred during the morning, resulting in reduced visibility in blowing dirt at some locations across eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial report was from public, and later confirmed throughout the morning by law enforcement, fire/rescue, cooperative observer and trained spotters. Most of the county experienced low to no visibility in blowing dirt through the morning as winds gusted 50 to 70 mph. Newspaper reported highway 40/287 was closed and numerous minor injury accidents which involved four semis and six cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third day of high winds occurred as a slow-moving but intense weather system moved over the Central High Plains. Peak winds of 55 to 65 mph occurred during the morning, resulting in reduced visibility in blowing dirt at some locations across eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds increased around sunrise and continued through much of the afternoon with a peak gust of 71 mph reported at a mesonet site northeast of Kit Carson. Another unofficial report of 80 mph was also received from a handheld instrument southeast of Kit Carson at 730 am MDT.  The local newspaper mentioned a one-vehicle rollover accident occurred as a result of strong winds at 700 am MDT.  Numerous other vehicle accidents occurred during the day, primarily attributed to the dust storm conditions which accompanied the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third day of high winds occurred as a slow-moving but intense weather system moved over the Central High Plains. Peak winds of 55 to 65 mph occurred during the morning, resulting in reduced visibility in blowing dirt at some locations across eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds increased after sunrise and continued into the afternoon with a peak gust of 71 mph reported at a mesonet site in Seibert.  Wind gusts to 68 mph occurred at the Burlington ASOS.  Trees were reported down in Burlington and roof damage to a barn was reported 16 miles south of Flagler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the peak of snowmelt on the Grand Mesa was a period of heavy rainfall which may have combined to precipitate a massive landslide on the north side of the Grand Mesa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide consisting of about 30 million cubic meters of rock and soil broke loose from the side of the Grand Mesa near the 10,000 foot level and traveled nearly 3 miles down the West Salt Creek drainage.  Three ranchers were apparently buried by the landslide which deposited debris over 100 feet deep along the slide path.  This was one of the largest landslides ever documented in the United States.  This landslide occurred during the period of peak snowmelt and just after heavy rainfall occurred in the area.  Radar estimates and nearby rain gauge measurements for the 36 hour period prior to the landslide were around one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed little change from June. Drought conditions continued in southern Cheyenne County where a Severe (D2) drought rating continued through the month of August. Observers near Wild Horse and Cheyenne Wells reported rainfall one to one and a half inches above normal for the month, but further south and east, precipitation was once again below normal.

Heavy snow produced avalanche conditions to the central Colorado mountains in late January. Back country slides were likely with a few of these impacting travel over secondary and well traveled roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Park Cone chute near Taylor Reservoir ran about noon mile marker 18 along Forest Road 742. The slide at this location was around 14 feet deep. Slides as large as 150 yards across occurred over an area 2 miles long near the Park Cone chute.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hard slab, skier-triggered, avalanche occurred in the East Vail Chutes just outside the Vail ski area. The slide was of medium size and broke into old snow layers, the crown face was near 10 feet in depth at the center. This avalanche was 900 feet wide and ran 1463 vertical feet. Four skiers were trapped in the slide which injured 2 people and killed another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche was triggered by a group of skiers in the East Vail Chutes. This medium sized slide injured two of the skiers and killed another. Trees up to 12 inches in diameter were also snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm produced locally heavy rainfall rate and caused flash flooding at  low water crossings in western Pueblo County.

A flash flood occurred on Bear Creek on the north face of East Spanish Peak.  The flood waters moved through the Spanish Peaks Scout Ranch.  The evacuation of 96 people was accomplished through the flood waters.  A cabin and maintenance building were flooded.  No one was seriously injured.

A nearly stationary strong thunderstorm produced flash flooding in the Ellicott and Yoder areas.  Low lying areas and the Brackett and Big Springs Creek watersheds were flooded, along with adjacent county roads and Highway 94.
A severe thunderstorms caused tree damage in Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dead tree was struck by lightning then blow down by severe winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms moved southeast from the Akron area during the evening and ultimately formed a line of storms extending from Burlington southwest to near Kit Carson. Strong winds to 63 mph were observed by the ASOS site at the Kit Carson County airport. The strong outflow winds resulted in blowing dirt which briefly reduced visibility to near zero in a few areas.  Six utility poles were blown down on Hwy 385 south of Burlington, and tree damage was also reported at a nearby farmstead.

Severe thunderstorms producing damaging straight-line winds, large hail and tornadoes moved across northern Lincoln County.  The wind and large hail caused extensive damage.  In Hugo, a detached garage and a small shed were blown over and a roof was blown off a trailer home.  Two classic cars in the garage were damaged. Extensive tree damage occurred with large limbs broken off trees throughout town.  Scattered damage to north facing windows was also observed with more isolated damage to shingles, gutters and vehicle windows.  A truck was blown over just southeast of Hugo, causing minor injuries to the two occupants.  Hail  damage was reported in the northern part of Limon, east of Hugo and in Genoa.  The majority damage to windows and siding occurred on the north facing sides of the homes and buildings.  The hail also injured a number of cattle northeast of Hugo.  Four tornadoes were observed but only one produced minor damage.

An Arctic cold front associated with a strong storm system over the northern Rockies blasted into northeastern Colorado.  The temperature in Denver plummeted from the high of 64 degrees at 4:37 pm on the 10th to 34 degrees by 5:53 pm. The temperatures continued to fall and dropped into the teens by early evening.  The Arctic airmass remained entrenched over the region, with the high temperatures failing to reach the teens from the 11th through the 13th.  This was the first time since records have been taken in Denver, dating back to  1872, that there were three consecutive days with the maximum temperatures remained below 20 degrees in the month of November.  Several temperature records were broken.  In Denver alone, a|record low max of 16 degrees on the 11th broke the previous record of 19 set back in 1916.  A record low max of 6 degrees on the 12th broke the previous record of 9 degrees, also set in 1916.  The record low of -13 on the 12th, shattered the previous record of -4 degrees set back in 1872.  Even more impressive, the record low of -14 degrees on the 13th broke the previous record of -3 degrees set back in 1916.  The sudden drop in temperatures on the 10th, followed by several days of sub freezing temperatures chilled trees to the core. A record drop in temperatures preceded by mild fall temperatures damaged or killed scores of trees across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  At the time, the trees were not finished with the hardening process that enables them to withstand winter weather.  Many were  instantly flash-frozen, and some would not show signs of damage until spring. Of the 2.2 million trees in the city of Denver alone, tens of thousands were killed from the November frost.

Severe thunderstorms producing damaging straight-line winds, large hail and tornadoes moved across northern Lincoln County.  The wind and large hail caused extensive damage.  In Hugo, a detached garage and a small shed were blown over and a roof was blown off a trailer home.  Two classic cars in the garage were damaged. Extensive tree damage occurred with large limbs broken off trees throughout town.  Scattered damage to north facing windows was also observed with more isolated damage to shingles, gutters and vehicle windows.  A truck was blown over just southeast of Hugo, causing minor injuries to the two occupants.  Hail  damage was reported in the northern part of Limon, east of Hugo and in Genoa.  The majority damage to windows and siding occurred on the north facing sides of the homes and buildings.  The hail also injured a number of cattle northeast of Hugo.  Four tornadoes were observed but only one produced minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail caused extensive window damage to a home.

Small stream flooding covered numerous county roads southwest of Fort Morgan. Some of the roads along Rock Creek that were temporarily closed included:  Morgan County Road 4, between CO 52 and MCR D; MCR1, between MCR D and MCR3; and MCR1, between MCR H and MCR 3. Minor flooding forced the closure of several county roads in southeast Weld County.  Some of the closures included:  Weld County Road 16, from WCR 71 to WCR73; WCR10, from WCR71 to WCR79; WCR4, from WCR73 to WCR81; WCR14, from WCR87 to WCR89; and WCR93, from WCR16 to WCR18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on top of already saturated soils produced extensive small stream flooding southwest of Fort Morgan.

Strong storms moved southeast from the Akron area during the evening and ultimately formed a line of storms extending from Burlington southwest to near Kit Carson. Strong winds to 63 mph were observed by the ASOS site at the Kit Carson County airport. The strong outflow winds resulted in blowing dirt which briefly reduced visibility to near zero in a few areas.  Six utility poles were blown down on Hwy 385 south of Burlington, and tree damage was also reported at a nearby farmstead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six utility poles were blown down by strong thunderstorm outflow at Hwy 385 and Road G south of Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced two brief landspouts.  In addition, a small boat was capsized by strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly on the southwest part of the Rocky Mountain Arsenal Wildlife Refuge.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One woman was killed and seven others injured by a lightning strike in Rocky Mountain National Park; she was the first lightning fatality in the park since 2000.  The incident occurred at the Ute Crossing Trail, just off of Trail Ridge Road between Rainbow Curve and Forest Canyon Overlook.  Six of the other victims suffered minor injuries.  One had to be hospitalized when he was knocked unconcious;  he suffered burns on his shoulder and scrapes to his face when he was knocked to the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning killed one person and injured seven others at RMNP.

During the afternoon a severe thunderstorm developed over East Central Colorado and moved eastward intensifying.   Widespread damaging winds occurred with this storm, with measured gusts up to 78 mph at the Burlington airport.  The widespread damaging winds broke off hundreds of power poles leading to widespread power outages for Kit Carson county and produced blowing dust dropping visibilities to under a mile briefly near Stratton.  A State of Emergency was declare for Kit Carson county due to the widespread power outage.  The largest hail reported was golf ball size northwest of Burlington.  Total damage estimates were just over $3 million for all the hail and wind events in the county, with the majority of damage resulting from loss of farm equipment and loss of crop watering equipment. This figure does not include crop loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to golfball size, across parts of Larimer, Morgan, Phillips and Morgan Counties.  One tornado touched down briefly in an open field.  In Douglas and Elbert Counties, lightning sparked three fires in the vicinities of Franktown and Elizabeth in the span of ten minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an outbuilding south of Elizabeth, causing moderate damage to the contents inside.  Around the same time, lightning struck a satellite dish at a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to golfball size, across parts of Jefferson, Elbert and Lincoln Counties.

An Arctic cold front associated with a strong storm system over the northern Rockies blasted into northeastern Colorado.  The temperature in Denver plummeted from the high of 64 degrees at 4:37 pm on the 10th to 34 degrees by 5:53 pm. The temperatures continued to fall and dropped into the teens by early evening.  The Arctic airmass remained entrenched over the region, with the high temperatures failing to reach the teens from the 11th through the 13th.  This was the first time since records have been taken in Denver, dating back to  1872, that there were three consecutive days with the maximum temperatures remained below 20 degrees in the month of November.  Several temperature records were broken.  In Denver alone, a|record low max of 16 degrees on the 11th broke the previous record of 19 set back in 1916.  A record low max of 6 degrees on the 12th broke the previous record of 9 degrees, also set in 1916.  The record low of -13 on the 12th, shattered the previous record of -4 degrees set back in 1872.  Even more impressive, the record low of -14 degrees on the 13th broke the previous record of -3 degrees set back in 1916.  The sudden drop in temperatures on the 10th, followed by several days of sub freezing temperatures chilled trees to the core. A record drop in temperatures preceded by mild fall temperatures damaged or killed scores of trees across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  At the time, the trees were not finished with the hardening process that enables them to withstand winter weather.  Many were  instantly flash-frozen, and some would not show signs of damage until spring. Of the 2.2 million trees in the city of Denver alone, tens of thousands were killed from the November frost.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew a trailer over near Elbert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing damaging straight-line winds, large hail and tornadoes moved across northern Lincoln County.  The wind and large hail caused extensive damage.  In Hugo, a detached garage and a small shed were blown over and a roof was blown off a trailer home.  Two classic cars in the garage were damaged. Extensive tree damage occurred with large limbs broken off trees throughout town.  Scattered damage to north facing windows was also observed with more isolated damage to shingles, gutters and vehicle windows.  A truck was blown over just southeast of Hugo, causing minor injuries to the two occupants.  Hail  damage was reported in the northern part of Limon, east of Hugo and in Genoa.  The majority damage to windows and siding occurred on the north facing sides of the homes and buildings.  The hail also injured a number of cattle northeast of Hugo.  Four tornadoes were observed but only one produced minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched in open country.  No damage was reported.

An Arctic cold front associated with a strong storm system over the northern Rockies blasted into northeastern Colorado.  The temperature in Denver plummeted from the high of 64 degrees at 4:37 pm on the 10th to 34 degrees by 5:53 pm. The temperatures continued to fall and dropped into the teens by early evening.  The Arctic airmass remained entrenched over the region, with the high temperatures failing to reach the teens from the 11th through the 13th.  This was the first time since records have been taken in Denver, dating back to  1872, that there were three consecutive days with the maximum temperatures remained below 20 degrees in the month of November.  Several temperature records were broken.  In Denver alone, a|record low max of 16 degrees on the 11th broke the previous record of 19 set back in 1916.  A record low max of 6 degrees on the 12th broke the previous record of 9 degrees, also set in 1916.  The record low of -13 on the 12th, shattered the previous record of -4 degrees set back in 1872.  Even more impressive, the record low of -14 degrees on the 13th broke the previous record of -3 degrees set back in 1916.  The sudden drop in temperatures on the 10th, followed by several days of sub freezing temperatures chilled trees to the core. A record drop in temperatures preceded by mild fall temperatures damaged or killed scores of trees across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  At the time, the trees were not finished with the hardening process that enables them to withstand winter weather.  Many were  instantly flash-frozen, and some would not show signs of damage until spring. Of the 2.2 million trees in the city of Denver alone, tens of thousands were killed from the November frost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening a line of northward moving storms progressed east across East Central Colorado.  Thunderstorm wind gusts of 59-84 MPH were reported with the line of storms over the area.  The strongest wind gust was reported 26 S of Flagler, with the second strongest gust of 76 MPH reported at the Yuma airport.  In addition a roof was blown off a house in Kit Carson where wind gusts were estimated to be 100 MPH.  Training storms over eastern Washington county during the afternoon lead to area flooding north of Otis.  The flood waters then moved into northwest Yuma county through the night and the following morning, with multiple locations of water over Highway 59 and County Road 54 near Clarkville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof blown off of a house in town.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon a severe thunderstorm developed over East Central Colorado and moved eastward intensifying.   Widespread damaging winds occurred with this storm, with measured gusts up to 78 mph at the Burlington airport.  The widespread damaging winds broke off hundreds of power poles leading to widespread power outages for Kit Carson county and produced blowing dust dropping visibilities to under a mile briefly near Stratton.  A State of Emergency was declare for Kit Carson county due to the widespread power outage.  The largest hail reported was golf ball size northwest of Burlington.  Total damage estimates were just over $3 million for all the hail and wind events in the county, with the majority of damage resulting from loss of farm equipment and loss of crop watering equipment. This figure does not include crop loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche caught a skier near Crested Butte, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were skiing down from the top of Schuykill Ridge when the snow on the slope fractured several feet above one skier and quickly picked up speed. Shortly after getting caught in the avalanche, the skier was carried into a tree and broke a leg on impact.  About a third of the way down the slope, the skier was able to stop with the uninjured leg and allowed the remaining moving snow to wash down the slope.  The soft-slab avalanche broke in old snow 1 to 2 feet deep near treeline at the 11,300 foot level, then ran approximately 550 vertical feet in a path that was about 25 feet wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to golfball size, and damaging winds across Arapahoe, Boulder, Elbert and Jefferson Counties.  Flash flooding was reported in central Jefferson County.   Heavy rainfall produced street flooding in Denver with some basement flooding reported as well.  The strong winds downed trees and power lines.  One man was injured by a nearby lightning strike in Arvada.  Lightning also sparked a small attic fire in Castle Pines and caused brief electrical outages in Denver.

A late season storm system produced a swath of wet snow over northeast Colorado, far northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska. Two to five inch snow amounts were common over Kit Carson and Yuma counties with a small area of greater than six inches of snow in far northwest Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulating on area roadways resulted in many slide offs and vehicle accidents Sunday resulting in only minor injuries. Several schools were closed Monday morning. Highway 34 west of Yuma was closed for a time Sunday morning after several jack knifed semi trailers and slide offs were reported. This resulted in a a line of vehicles backed up at the Yuma-Washington county line waiting for the road to re-open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather disturbance and its associated cold front moved across northern Colorado during the late morning and afternoon; spawning several tornadoes, damaging wind, large hail, very heavy rainfall.  Nine tornadoes touched down across northeast Colorado. Three of the tornadoes occurred in Park County, at elevations of 8,000-10,000 feet. The first tornado occurred 8 miles south-southwest of Fairplay; it damaged the roof of a residence and was assigned a rating of EF-1. The second tornado developed 6 miles southeast of Fairplay in open country. The third tornado developed 4 miles west of Lake George; it was given a rating of EF-2. The last tornado caused damage to some homes and overturned several recreational vehicles at an RV park. Several power lines were also downed and some buildings in the town of Lake George were damaged, and trees were also snapped from their bases.  This tornado then passed into Teller County.  ||In Aurora, another tornado touched down near the Blackstone Country Club, it lifted some golf carts and flipped an empty construction trailer. One of the carts was thrown on top of a caddy walking nearby; he was taken to a hospital with minor injuries. It was assigned an EF-1 rating. Five other tornadoes developed over open country across Douglas, Elbert and Weld Counties with little additional damage reported.  In Englewood, lightning struck a tree which damaged two nearby homes and a truck.  Large hail, up to half dollar size, very heavy rain, and minor flooding was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down and caused damage in an RV park.  Several power lines were downed and a some damage reported along Park County Road 370.  Some buildings in Lake George also sustained minor damage.  Trees were snapped from their bases.  The tornado continued its track into Teller County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough produced heavy snow and localized blizzard conditions in many mountain areas of western Colorado. Some higher elevation valleys also received significant snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 8 to 18 inches were measured across the area. Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting snow with localized blizzard conditions.  Even stronger winds at ridgetops included 79 mph on Eagle Mountain and 72 mph on Kendall Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of the 30th, high based showers and thunderstorms produced microburst winds over portions of northern Jefferson, eastern Boulder and southwest Weld Counties.  Peak wind reports included:  77 mph at White Ranch Open Space; 72 mph, 2 miles north of Longmont; 67 mph, 1 mile south-southwest of Erie and Firestone; 60 mph, 3 miles south of Mead and 59 mph, 5 miles northwest of Henderson.  The wind downed power lines in Weld County which sparked a small fire.||High winds then developed over parts of the Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains through the morning  of the 31st.  Peak wind gusts included:  98 mph, 2 miles south-southeast of Gold Hill; 77 mph, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 63 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Loveland; 62 mph at Cedar Point; 61 mph at Akron Airport, Centennial, and Fort Morgan Airport; 60 mph at Holyoke and 1 mile southwest of Windsor;  59 mph at Buckley AFB; and 58 mph, 1 mile east of Greeley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Weld County Roads 31 and 66, downed power lines started a small fire in some trees.

On the evening of the 30th, high based showers and thunderstorms produced microburst winds over portions of northern Jefferson, eastern Boulder and southwest Weld Counties.  Peak wind reports included:  77 mph at White Ranch Open Space; 72 mph, 2 miles north of Longmont; 67 mph, 1 mile south-southwest of Erie and Firestone; 60 mph, 3 miles south of Mead and 59 mph, 5 miles northwest of Henderson.  The wind downed power lines in Weld County which sparked a small fire.||High winds then developed over parts of the Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains through the morning  of the 31st.  Peak wind gusts included:  98 mph, 2 miles south-southeast of Gold Hill; 77 mph, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 63 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Loveland; 62 mph at Cedar Point; 61 mph at Akron Airport, Centennial, and Fort Morgan Airport; 60 mph at Holyoke and 1 mile southwest of Windsor;  59 mph at Buckley AFB; and 58 mph, 1 mile east of Greeley.

Frontier Airlines canceled 16 flights, when several planes sustained hail damage.  Forty flights at Denver International Airport were either delayed or canceled. Parts of Tower Road and Peoria Street, and other roads in Commerce City, were closed through the late evening hours, because of standing water.  Several vehicles stalled in standing water where water pooled in the underpasses.

Severe thunderstorms occurred in and around the Denver area.  The storms produced large hail, from quarter to golfball size.  Frontier Airlines canceled 16 flights, when several planes sustained hail damage.  Forty flights at Denver International Airport were either delayed or canceled. Some homes in Green Valley Ranch east of Denver were damaged by hail.  Several vehicles were also damaged.  Five short lived tornadoes touched down but no damage was reported.  ||The storms also produced heavy rain and strong damaging winds.  The wind downed trees and power lines east of Denver.  The heavy rain and hail, up to 6 inches deep in spots, caused street flooding around the metro area.  The hailstorm at Dick���s Sporting Goods Park knocked out power to the stadium where the 4A and 5A girls soccer championship games were about to be played.  The inclement weather snarled traffic on Interstate 70, Pe��a Boulevard, U.S. 85 and elsewhere due to the flooded roads. In some areas, snow plows were called out to clear the hail.  Parts of Tower Road and Peoria Street, and other roads in Commerce City, were closed through the late evening hours, because of standing water.  Several vehicles stalled in standing water where water pooled in the underpasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines.

One man was killed, and four others injured, when they were struck by lightning in Rocky Mountain National Park.  The incident occurred near Rainbow Curve along Trail Ridge Road, not far from where another woman was struck and killed by lightning approximately 26 hours earlier.  Flash flooding was observed in Douglas and Elbert Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over three inches of heavy rain caused flash flooding north and northwest of Elizabeth.  The rainfall flooded barns and fields, and inundated the surrounding roadways with standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred in and around the Denver area.  The storms produced large hail, from quarter to golfball size.  Frontier Airlines canceled 16 flights, when several planes sustained hail damage.  Forty flights at Denver International Airport were either delayed or canceled. Some homes in Green Valley Ranch east of Denver were damaged by hail.  Several vehicles were also damaged.  Five short lived tornadoes touched down but no damage was reported.  ||The storms also produced heavy rain and strong damaging winds.  The wind downed trees and power lines east of Denver.  The heavy rain and hail, up to 6 inches deep in spots, caused street flooding around the metro area.  The hailstorm at Dick���s Sporting Goods Park knocked out power to the stadium where the 4A and 5A girls soccer championship games were about to be played.  The inclement weather snarled traffic on Interstate 70, Pe��a Boulevard, U.S. 85 and elsewhere due to the flooded roads. In some areas, snow plows were called out to clear the hail.  Parts of Tower Road and Peoria Street, and other roads in Commerce City, were closed through the late evening hours, because of standing water.  Several vehicles stalled in standing water where water pooled in the underpasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly.  No damage was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted from 30 mph to 50 mph across the zone for much of the day.  Shortly after noon, a peak wind gust of 67 mph was measured at the Pinto RAWS site.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jetstream coupled with abundant moisture from the Pacific brought an extended period of heavy snow to the North Central Mountains of Colorado where enhanced banded snowfall allowed storm totals to exceed 2 feet over a 3-day period.  Across the Front Range Foothills, strong downslope winds in excess of 80 mph preceded the storm.  Locally heavy snowfall eventually spilled over into northern foothills and adjacent plains.  Across the plains, the heaviest snowfall generally occurred north of Interstate 70.  Storm totals across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains ranged from 5 to 11 inches, with the heaviest snowfall in central Weld and northern Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  7.5 inches, 1 mile west of Northglenn; 5 inches, 5 miles northeast of Westminster; 4.5 inches, 5 miles west-northwest of Brighton; with 2 to 4 inches elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning a cold front with high winds accompanying it moved through Yuma county.  Wind gusts of 60 MPH were reported at Yuma with the frontal passage.  The winds then declined some until the late morning when a gust of 62 MPH was reported at Akron.  The area of high winds then moved across the southwest part of Yuma county into Kit Carson county where the Burlington airport reported a gust of 62 MPH in the early afternoon.  The winds then declined through the rest of the day.  There were some small areas of blowing dust, but these were confined to fields that had been tilled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at 1 W of Yuma and at the Wray airport with the initial frontal passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning a cold front with high winds accompanying it moved through Yuma county.  Wind gusts of 60 MPH were reported at Yuma with the frontal passage.  The winds then declined some until the late morning when a gust of 62 MPH was reported at Akron.  The area of high winds then moved across the southwest part of Yuma county into Kit Carson county where the Burlington airport reported a gust of 62 MPH in the early afternoon.  The winds then declined through the rest of the day.  There were some small areas of blowing dust, but these were confined to fields that had been tilled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the Burlington airport.  The peak gust lasted for roughly 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter storm generated heavy snow across portions of Lake and Chaffee Counties.  Snowfall amounts across the higher elevations were well over a foot, but Monarch Ski Area (Chaffee County) tallied  nearly 31 inches of snow.  Leadville (Lake County), and Wolf Creek Pass (Mineral County) recorded 11 inches of snow over a 48-hour period.

An intensifying storm system across southeast Colorado generated heavy snow in combination with gusty northerly winds at times.  Generally, around 6 inches accumulated across the warning area in the southeast counties. Lesser amounts occurred outside the warning area...including...4 to 5 inches near Pueblo Reservoir and Beulah (Pueblo County), Monument and Colorado Springs (El Paso County), Trinidad (Las Animas County), La Junta and Cheraw (Otero County).  Finally, 12 inches of snow was measured at Wolf Creek Pass in Mineral county.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jetstream coupled with abundant moisture from the Pacific brought an extended period of heavy snow to the North Central Mountains of Colorado where enhanced banded snowfall allowed storm totals to exceed 2 feet over a 3-day period.  Across the Front Range Foothills, strong downslope winds in excess of 80 mph preceded the storm.  Locally heavy snowfall eventually spilled over into northern foothills and adjacent plains.  Across the plains, the heaviest snowfall generally occurred north of Interstate 70.  Storm totals across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains ranged from 5 to 11 inches, with the heaviest snowfall in central Weld and northern Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  32.5 inches, 2 miles south-southeast of Breckenridge; 25.5 inches at Fremont Pass, 19 inches, 9 miles east-northeast of Dillon; 18 inches at Copper Mountain, 16 inches, 1 mile east of Dillon and 7 miles south-southwest of Frisco; 15 inches at Elliot Ridge, Fremont Pass and Hoosier Pass; 14 inches, north of Fairplay, 13 inches at Jackwhacker Gulch and 11 inches at Winter Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall total of 1.26 inches fell within a two hour period, with most of the precipitation having occurred in less than one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough crossed the region and was followed by a moist northwest flow aloft which resulted in significant to heavy snowfall.  Strong winds combined with the snowfall in some areas which resulted in blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 7 to 9 inches were measured across the area.  Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph produced some areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe storms caused hail up to the size of half dollars and wind gust from 60 to 70 mph across the I-25 corridor and southeast plains.

A storm system that moved through the area produced large hail, up to golfball size, and street flooding in parts of the metro area then spread east into the plains.  The hailstorm was the most costly of the summer season with with insured losses topping $213.3 million, according to the Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Association.|Insurance claims included 29,297 automobile claims worth more than $87.2 million and 14,287 property claims for $126 million, ranking the storm as the eighth most expensive to hit the state.  The storm also caused multiple accidents. A semi became detached from a trailer, blocking traffic on westbound Interstate 70 west of Tower Road.  Downed power poles blocked a roadway on CO 79 near Bennett, at mile marker 10.  In addition to the damaging winds, the storms produced large hail, from nickel to golfball size.  The intense thunderstorm winds also downed trees near Fort Morgan.  Flash flooding was reported over parts of northern Washington County.  The combination of flash flooding and an accident involving a semi-trailer forced the closure of CO 61 for a several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorm winds downed some trees in Fort Morgan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to golfball size, and damaging winds across Arapahoe, Boulder, Elbert and Jefferson Counties.  Flash flooding was reported in central Jefferson County.   Heavy rainfall produced street flooding in Denver with some basement flooding reported as well.  The strong winds downed trees and power lines.  One man was injured by a nearby lightning strike in Arvada.  Lightning also sparked a small attic fire in Castle Pines and caused brief electrical outages in Denver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall, nearly two inches in one hour, flooded several residences.  In addition, several bridges along Forest Estate Road were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from ping pong to golfball size, in Logan and Washington Counties.  Damaging winds were also observed around Sterling.
A severe thunderstorm produced wind gusts to 62 mph in northeast Elbert County.
A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size, along with wind gusts to 66 mph in northern Douglas County.
A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 60 mph near Windsor.

Active monsoonal conditions brought heavy rainfall to many parts of western Colorado on the 4th. Numerous roads were washed out and debris flows caused some traffic delays. Hail to the size of quarters accumulated to the depth of 2 inches in the Redlands of the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 65 was closed near mile marker 56 due to 1 foot of flowing sediment from the swollen Plateau Creek which over-flowed.

One tornado touched down briefly in an open field.  In Douglas and Elbert Counties, lightning sparked three fires in the vicinities of Franktown and Elizabeth in the span of ten minutes.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts to around 65 mph, across Larimer, Lincoln and Logan Counties.  Heavy rain produced localized flash flooding in Larimer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced localized flash flooding in the High Park burn area.  Mud debris flowed across CO 14 at mile marker 106.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced flash flooding over northeast Larimer and west central Weld Counties.  Localized street flooding was also observed in south Denver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, up to 2.4 inches, produced flooding on O Street, west of Weld County Road 33.  In Windsor, extensive street flooding inundated homes and stalled several cars; forcing numerous road closures until the floodwaters subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of strong thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and moved northeast across Kit Carson and Yuma counties through the evening hours.  All severe reports received from spotters with this event were large hail with no wind reports logged.
Numerous strong storms brought heavy rain and isolated flash flooding to western El Paso County during the evening.

Numerous severe storms occurred across the I-25 corridor and southeast plains producing hail up to two inches in diameter.  Two tornadoes occurred over open country east of Trinidad, causing damage to trees, fences, power lines, and one outbuilding.

Severe thunderstorms broke out across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains of Colorado.  The storms produced four tornadoes in Washington, Weld and Phillips Counties.  No damage was observed.  Elsewhere, large hail, up to 1.5 inches in diameter, was reported along with wind gusts to 70 mph.
Severe storms produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls and some flooding in Ute Pass.

Damaging hail, nearly baseball size, caused extensive damage to homes and automobiles over portions of Arapahoe and Larimer Counties.  Localized flash flooding also occurred near Timnath in eastern Larimer County.

Damaging hail, nearly baseball size, caused extensive damage to homes and automobiles over portions of Arapahoe and Larimer Counties.  Localized flash flooding also occurred near Timnath in eastern Larimer County.

A few clusters of thunderstorms developed over Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties during the afternoon and moved east and southeast.  Several reports of penny to quarter size hail were received from spotters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea to penny size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging hail, nearly baseball size, caused extensive damage to homes and automobiles over portions of Arapahoe and Larimer Counties.  Localized flash flooding also occurred near Timnath in eastern Larimer County.

A severe thunderstorm produced intense straight line winds in Larimer County.  Over one thousand trees were blown down at the Bellair Campground near Red Feather Lakes.  The tree damage stretched across a 36-acre area.  In addition, power lines were downed near Molly Lake Trailhead, approximately mile marker 4 on Larimer County Road 69, and fences and signs were damaged. Early season subtropical moisture and daytime heating resulted in scattered afternoon thunderstorms over the mountains of southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two ultramarathon runners from Canada were struck by lightning on Handies Peak at about the 14,050 foot level while competing in the 100-mile Handrock Endurance Run.  Both men incurred minor injuries.  The headlamp of one runner exploded when struck by the lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture from the southwest resulted in thunderstorms across portions of western Colorado with heavy rainfall that caused some flash flooding and mud slides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half inch of rain within a half hour produced minor flooding and mudslides onto County Road 204 below the Junction Creek Campground.  Culverts under the road became clogged with a large amount of debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture from the southwest resulted in thunderstorms across portions of western Colorado with heavy rainfall that caused some flash flooding and mud slides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous mud slides up to a foot deep were reported north of Redstone due to heavy rainfall and runoff. Southbound lanes of Highway 133 were blocked for several hours as road crews removed the debris.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of July started out hot and dry but by the middle of the month the Southwest Monsoon season had begun. Although the majority of the climate reporting stations measured deficits in monthly precipitation totals, a few sites experienced significant rain showers which resulted in monthly totals above normal. The drought worsened a bit in the far northwest corner of the state, but the bigger impact was in the Four Corners region where extreme drought conditions spread into the state by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained entrenched across the lower elevations of the Four Corners region of Colorado through the month. However, extreme conditions developed in the far southern portion of this zone by the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained entrenched across the lower elevations of the Animas River Basin of southwest Colorado through the month. However, extreme conditions developed in the far southern portion of this zone by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in western Mesa County near the Colorado National Monument produced flash flooding in the Redlands area of Grand Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No Thoroughfare Canyon at the Tabeguache trailhead had fast flowing flood waters over 8 inches deep flowing through it for at least a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in western Mesa County near the Colorado National Monument produced flash flooding in the Redlands area of Grand Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall, with 1.6 inches measured within about an hour in the Redlands, caused numerous dry washes to run with fast moving flood waters. Waterfalls came off the Colorado National Monument with comments from local residents that they were the most seen in over 14 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded disturbance in a northwest flow acted on residual moisture to develop a strong thunderstorm in the Steamboat Springs area.
An embedded disturbance in a northwest flow acted on residual moisture to develop a strong thunderstorm in the Steamboat Springs area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was measured 1.5 miles north of the Strawberry Park Elementary school on County Road 36.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded disturbance in a northwest flow acted on residual moisture to develop a strong thunderstorm in the Steamboat Springs area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind blown hail stripped a lot of leaves off trees and accumulated to a depth of two inches.  The largest hail stones were the sized of ping pong balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture flowed up into southwest Colorado and resulted in some thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding within various locations in and near Pagosa Springs.  Flowing water about 18 inches deep ran across Highway 160 about a half mile east of the junction with Highway 84.  A stream which comes down to Rumbaugh Road in northeast Pagosa Springs had a high volume of water which ran across the road and continued onto Highway 160; with water 6 to 8 inches deep in an area generally bounded on the east and west ends by 1st Street and 2nd Street and bounded on the north and south ends by Lewis Street and Highway 160.  Another area of flash flooding occurred on the west end of town in the Bastille Drive area where up to a foot of water depth was reported.  Water damage occurred to a number of residences and businesses, including an auto supply store which still had about 12 inches of standing water inside the store the next morning.  The river gauge in the San Juan River within Pagosa Springs measured a stage rise of just over 3 feet in less than 1.5 hours.  The river rose up to the top of the banks in some places but did not overflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in southwest Archuleta County during the afternoon of July 16th. A nearby stationary front resulted in a series of thunderstorms that moved across the same general location in the northwest flow aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage occurred to County Road 500 that was washed out between mile markers 20 and 30 due to a flash flood.  The flooding was isolated and included a significant debris flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active monsoon thunderstorms produced another afternoon of heavy rainfall in southwest Colorado where numerous debris flows were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms moved over the Hermosa area and caused a large mudslide to close a forest service road for a few hours northwest of Durango.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active monsoon thunderstorms produced another afternoon of heavy rainfall in southwest Colorado where numerous debris flows were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large mudslide was reported to be at least 6 inches deep and prevented travel at the intersection of Florida Road and North College Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active monsoon thunderstorms produced another afternoon of heavy rainfall in southwest Colorado where numerous debris flows were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous mudslides were reported along Highway 145 between Placerville and Sawpit and disrupted traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Lake City area during the afternoon of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall near Lake City with amounts of over and inch caused flash flooding which closed Highway 149 and deposited a large amount of debris onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collapsing thunderstorm in the Animas River Valley produced microburst winds that caused isolated damage in the community of Rockwood. Rainfall from thunderstorms in the area also caused a large debris flow to close nearby Highway 550 in both directions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 200 foot section of Highway 550 was closed due to flash flooding which deposited a significant amount of debris onto the road near the turnoff to Tamarron Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collapsing thunderstorm in the Animas River Valley produced microburst winds that caused isolated damage in the community of Rockwood. Rainfall from thunderstorms in the area also caused a large debris flow to close nearby Highway 550 in both directions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles and trailers were damaged from strong thunderstorm microburst at a residence in the community of Rockwood.  One trailer was lifted about 10 feet off the ground by the strong winds. A house and other buildings about 100 yards away from the impacted vehicles were not damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon thunderstorm outbreak caused several flash floods and debris flows across western Colorado where there were numerous reports of rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 70 was closed in both directions due to debris that was deposited on the roadway from flash flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon thunderstorm outbreak caused several flash floods and debris flows across western Colorado where there were numerous reports of rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to six inches of fast moving water ran across Highway 64 west of Meeker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon thunderstorm outbreak caused several flash floods and debris flows across western Colorado where there were numerous reports of rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several areas of flash flooding occurred along and over Highway 141 southwest of Whitewater which prompted the closing of the highway for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon thunderstorm outbreak caused several flash floods and debris flows across western Colorado where there were numerous reports of rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought an area of heavy rainfall from Hotchkiss to areas north of Cedaredge. Flash flooding impacted portions of Hotchkiss and closed a portion of Highway 65 north of Cedaredge which deposited a large amount of debris onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon thunderstorm outbreak caused several flash floods and debris flows across western Colorado where there were numerous reports of rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall moved slowly over the Tavaputs Plateau near Baxter Pass. Flood waters made many areas of Colorado Road 202 impassable, especially  where branches of West Salt Creek traverse the roadway.  A bridge embankment was damaged from the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated ground conditions from several days of active monsoon thunderstorms led to early morning flooding in portions of Mesa County after a round of late night thunderstorms moved across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along the West Salt Creek basin produced flash flooding along West Salt Creek.  Flood waters extended about 60 feet beyond outside of the creek bank at a location approximately 5 miles northwest of Mack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated ground conditions from several days of active monsoon thunderstorms led to early morning flooding in portions of Mesa County after a round of late night thunderstorms moved across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed at the intersection of Highway 65 and the DeBeque cutoff as well as other areas to the south of DeBeque where farmland was inundated.  Irrigation ditches overflowed and were filled with debris.  Many roads in the area were inundated and isolated some residents by cutting off escape routes.  Some flood damage occurred to outbuildings near one of the houses.
